title: Making a magnetic secondary glazing panel
subtitle: A lightweight removable secondary pane held by magnetic strips on a steel frame
excerpt: A magnetic secondary glazing system uses a thin acrylic or glass sheet held to a steel channel frame by a compression magnetic strip. It is removable for summer ventilation and costs 80 to 150 pounds per window depending on size. This is the measurement, frame-cutting, and fitting sequence for a sash or casement window.
sourceNotes: Histoglass and Selectaglaze secondary glazing technical installation guides (publicly available). BFRC guidance on secondary glazing energy performance. Listed Buildings England guidance on reversible draught improvement for historic windows.

glossaryTerms.definition: An additional glazed panel fitted to the room side of an existing window to create a still-air gap between the panes, improving thermal performance and reducing noise without replacing the original window.

The thermal transmittance of a building element, measured in W/m²K. Lower values indicate better insulation. A single-glazed sash window may have a U-value of 5.0; adding secondary glazing with a 50 mm gap reduces this to around 2.0.

content.text: The premise of magnetic
secondary glazing

is a thin pressed-steel channel screwed to the window reveal, with a flexible magnetic strip bonded to the face. The acrylic or glass sheet has a matching strip bonded to its edge. The panel snaps to the frame, is removed in a few seconds by peeling the magnetic edge, and stores behind a wardrobe or under a bed in summer. A correctly fitted 50 mm air gap between the original pane and the secondary sheet reduces the combined
U-value
from around 5.0 to 2.0 W/m²K, cutting heat loss through the window by more than half.
What you will need
Pressed-steel magnetic secondary glazing channel, supplied in kit form or by the metre. Measure the reveal depth: you need at least 55 mm of flat reveal to mount the frame.
4 mm clear acrylic sheet, cut to 5 mm less than the internal frame dimensions on all four sides
Self-adhesive magnetic strip, 20 mm wide, for both the frame face and the acrylic edge
10 x 25 mm countersunk screws for the channel; plastic wall plugs if fixing to masonry
Contact adhesive primer for the magnetic strip-to-acrylic bond
Measuring

Measure the window reveal opening at three points: top, middle, and bottom for width; left, centre, and right for height. Use the smallest measurement in each direction. A Victorian window reveal is rarely square or parallel; working from the smallest dimension means the frame will fit without binding.
Cutting and assembling the frame
Cut the steel channel to length with a mitre saw fitted with a metal-cutting blade. Mitre the corners at 45 degrees. Steel channel cuts cleanly; deburr cut edges with a file.
Dry-fit the four sections in the reveal to confirm the mitre joints close without gaps. The frame should sit 3 to 5 mm back from the plane of the existing window.
Fix the frame using a screw every 300 mm. Drill pilot holes in the channel first; the steel is thin enough to split if you drive a screw without a pilot hole.
Wipe the front face of the frame with isopropyl alcohol. Apply the self-adhesive magnetic strip continuously around the face, pressing firmly and using a roller on the straight sections.

Apply adhesive primer to the acrylic edge. Bond the matching magnetic strip to the edge, taking care to align polarity. Mark the top of the panel with a pencil before removing the protective backing so you can re-fit it correctly every time.

Snap the panel to the frame. Press around the perimeter. The magnetic compression should hold the panel flush without gaps. On a cold night, run your hand around the frame edge: no cold air should be detectable.
Listed buildings and rental properties

Magnetic secondary glazing does not need listed building consent in most cases. It is fully reversible: no adhesive touches historic fabric, the screw holes are minimal, and the panel leaves no trace when removed. Check with your local authority conservation officer before fitting if your building is Grade I listed.

attrs.symptom: Panel pulls away from frame in the corners

content.text: Acrylic warps slightly with temperature change. Extend the magnetic strip to within 10 mm of each corner to increase pull force at the ends.

attrs.symptom: Condensation on the inside of the secondary pane

content.text: The gap is too airtight, trapping moisture-laden air in the void. Drill two 5 mm holes at the bottom corners of the frame to allow very slow air circulation. This reduces the condensation trap without compromising most of the thermal performance.

attrs.symptom: Frame not square; panel edges show a gap on one side

content.text: Pack the low side of the frame with self-adhesive draught-seal foam tape before bonding the magnetic strip. A 3 mm pack is usually enough to bring the frame into plane.
